narrative: Your law school application was denied because our predictive model, which evaluates the likelihood of passing the bar exam on the first try, estimated a 61.9% chance that you would not pass—above the threshold we use for admission decisions. This prediction draws from patterns in academic records, test scores, and backgrounds of past students, focusing on factors like grades, LSAT performance, and family circumstances that reliably signal bar success. The three biggest influences on your specific outcome were your Year 3 school decile ranking, your undergraduate GPA, and your LSAT score, in that order of impact. Most critically, your third-year decile of 3—well below the average of about 6 for those who passed—strongly pushed the model toward predicting failure, as it suggests challenges in maintaining high performance later in your studies. On a brighter note, your undergraduate GPA of 3.50 exceeded the 3.24 average among bar passers, which noticeably pulled the prediction in the direction of success and helped offset some concerns. Your LSAT score of 33, however, fell short of the typical 37-plus seen in successful students, contributing further toward the failure prediction by signaling potential hurdles in standardized legal reasoning. While your upper-middle family income aligns well with many who pass (around 45% of them), and your plan to attend full-time matches the norm for 93% of passers, these didn't carry enough weight to shift the overall outcome. Compared to admitted students, your profile shows strengths in early undergrad work but gaps in later grades and test performance that the model sees as higher-risk for the bar. This isn't a judgment on your potential or background—gender and race weren't factors here—but a data-driven flag on bar passage odds. To move forward, consider retaking the LSAT to boost your score closer to 37 or higher, and reflect on strategies to improve upper-year rankings, perhaps through tutoring or study groups. Strengthening these could dramatically improve your odds in a future application. Many students in similar positions have succeeded by targeting these areas, and we're rooting for your next steps.
